en.extract:
The fork-tailed storm petrel (Hydrobates furcatus) is a small seabird of the storm petrel family Hydrobatidae. It is the second-most abundant and widespread storm petrel (after  Leach's storm petrel) and is the only bird in its family that is bluish-grey in colour.
The fork-tailed storm petrel is pelagic, spending up to 8 months in the northern Pacific Ocean. They only return to land to breed, where they nest in a single colony. Their nests can be found in a rock crevice or small burrow, where a single egg is laid. Their breeding range is along the coast of the northern Pacific Ocean, extending from northern California to northeast Asia.
They mainly feed on planktonic crustaceans, small fish, and squid, but also consume offal. Similarly to other storm petrels, they forage by picking food off the surface of the water while in flight.


Taxonomy
The fork-tailed storm petrel was formally described in 1789 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the petrels in the genus Procellaria and coined the binomial name Procellaria furcata. Gmelin based his description on the "fork-tailed petrel" that had been described in 1785 in separate publications by the English ornithologist John Latham and the Welsh naturalist Thomas Pennant. It is now one of 18 species placed in the genus Hydrobates that was erected in 1822 by the German zoologist Friedrich Boie. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek  hudro- meaning "water-" with batēs meaning "walker". The specific epithet furcatus is Latin meaning "forked".
Two subspecies are recognized:

H. f. furcatus (Gmelin, JF, 1789) – northern population, breeds in northeast Asia to Alaska
H. f. plumbeus (Peale, 1849)  – southern population, breeds in southeast Alaska south to northern California
The major differences between the two subspecies are slight size and plumage differences, where southern populations appear somewhat smaller and darker. Southern populations also begin and end their breeding season earlier than their northern counterparts. However, no molecular genetic analysis has been conducted to discern the two subspecies.
The species was formerly assigned to the genus Oceanodroma before that genus was synonymized with Hydrobates.


Description

The fork-tailed storm petrel is small seabird that is 20 cm in length with a wingspan of 46 cm. Its foraging behaviour resembles other storm petrels, where it flies with short, stiff wingbeats close to the surface of the water.
Despite its name, the forked tail in this bird is not always visible. The distinguishing feature of fork-tailed storm petrels is their overall bluish-gray plumage. They generally have a paler underside, which contrasts the darker colours under their wings. They also have a dark gray forehead, a black ear patch, and a small, black bill. Other storm petrels are significantly darker, such as the similar looking ashy storm petrel. However, fork-tailed storm petrels from southern populations are somewhat darker than those in the north.
Males and females are generally very similar in colour and size. Juveniles also resemble adults with the exception of having a less noticeable notch in their tails.
They are generally silent and only call when entering their breeding colony at night. Their most common call is a 3- to 5-note, raspy "ana-ana-ana", heard from both sexes. This is especially intense during courtship, where their calls are accompanied by elaborate aerial displays. A higher-pitched, single note call is typically emitted by males, and it is used to locate females in noisy colonies and to maintain the pair bond.


Distribution and habitat

The range of the fork-tailed storm petrel is estimated to cover 22,400,000 km2 with over 6,000,000 individuals occurring globally, making it the second-most widespread and abundant storm petrel.  During the breeding season, the fork-tailed storm petrel form dense colonies on islands throughout northern California, Oregon, Washington, British Columbia, Alaska, and northeast Asia off the Kamchatka Peninsula. Most of their population is concentrated in Alaska, notably in the Bering Sea near the Aleutian Islands. Their southernmost colony is on Little River Rock in Humboldt County, with a population of 200 individuals. It was reported in July 1939 that fork-tailed storm petrels were breeding on the Flannan Isles, Na h-Eileanan Flannach, which are about 35 km west of the northern tip of the Outer Hebrides, Scotland.
Their nesting habitat varies, ranging from bare rock to forests. They typically build their nests under rock crevices or roots, or burrow into soft ground with low-growing vegetation.
As they frequent the open ocean, little is known about fork-tailed storm petrels outside of the breeding season. They have been frequently sighted off the Californian coast, making them the most northerly distributed storm petrel during the winter. Sparse sightings also indicate that they forage as far south as Hawaii.


Behaviour


Food and feeding
Like other storm petrels, the fork-tailed storm petrel mainly feeds on crustaceans and fish near the surface of the ocean, including amphipods, myctophids, shallow-water fish (such as greenling and sablefish), copepods, decapods, and squid. They are also extremely opportunistic and can be seen scavenging on fatty tissue of dead marine mammals and also trailing behind fishing boats.
Fork-tailed storm petrels have a well-developed olfactory system and heavily rely on odour to scout for food, so often they are the first birds to arrive at a pungent food source. When at the source, these birds seize their prey by fluttering across the water surface and may occasionally dive to depths of 0.6 m.
Like other Procellariiformes, the fork-tailed storm petrel produces stomach oil from its digested food and stores it in its proventriculus, a section of the bird's digestive system. This oil permits these birds to go for a long time without food, but also allows them to transport nutrient-rich food back to their chicks from distant sources.


Breeding

Like other species,  fork-tailed storm petrels spend most of their time out at sea and  only return to land to breed around late March to early April. To avoid predation and harassment by gulls, these birds only enter the colony at night and depart before sunrise.
The fork-tailed storm petrel builds its nest in rock crevices or small burrows on isolated islands. Courtship consists of vocalizations and aerial displays, often lasting for several weeks. Once established, pairs will remain together for the rest of the breeding season and if they are successful,  continually use the same nest year after year. Females will lay a single white egg directly on the floor of the burrow, and both parents will incubate it for around 50 days. Once the egg hatches, the adult  keeps the chick warm using its brood patch for the first five days. Once able to thermoregulate for itself, the parents  then leave the chick, only returning to feed it one every one to four nights. After two months of slow growth, the petrel chick will finally fledge and leave the burrow.

Living in areas with severe climatic conditions, fork-tailed storm petrels have many adaptations to ensure breeding success. Eggs can be left unattended  up to 7 days and still successfully hatch, whereas chick growth rates can be adjusted to being faster or slower depending on food supply, rather at a constant rate like many other birds.


Interspecific interactions
The fork-tailed storm petrel is often the sole prey item for predators early in the breeding season because they return to these remote islands long before any other seabirds. These birds comprise a significant part of the diet of river otters, gulls, and raptors. Further, introduced predators such as foxes, martens, and raccoons have a significant impact on breeding populations. To defend themselves, fork-tailed storm petrels can eject their stomach oil at an incoming threat.
Fork-tailed storm petrels generally coexist peacefully with other seabirds, where they can be seen sharing breeding habitat with tufted puffins. In some cases, aggression can be observed, where they are known to steal food from Leach's storm petrels.


Human impacts and conservation
Being widespread and abundant, the fork-tailed storm petrel is not threatened with extinction. However, humans impact their life history in many ways.
As a species that feeds on surface material and follows ships, this storm petrel often ingests oil and plastic with their food items. Surprisingly, the fork-tailed storm petrel is relatively unaffected by the toxicity of oil, as their natural diet contains substances that are very chemically similar. Plastics also do not severely affect the birds because they can be regurgitated after ingestion.
In contrast, being a top marine predator, fork-tailed storm petrels are susceptible to bioaccumulation. High lead concentrations have been found in the bones of petrels, and DDT can cause eggshells to become dangerously thin.
The greatest threats that face fork-tailed storm petrels today are global climate change and introduced species. An increased severity of storms makes  foraging more dangerous, decreasing the chances that an adult will return to the colony. The introduction of mammals also adversely affect storm petrels, where raccoons and river otters prey upon nests, and rabbits increase soil erosion and compromise the structural integrity of burrows.


References


External links
Fork-tailed storm petrel species account- Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Fork-tailed storm petrel identification-USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Fork-tailed storm petrel media- Internet Bird Collection
Fork-tailed storm petrel photo gallery- VIREO
Fork-tailed Storm-Petrel (Hydrobates furcatus)-Avibase

The '''fork-tailed storm petrel''' ('''''Hydrobates furcatus''''') is a small [[seabird]] of the [[storm petrel]] family [[Hydrobatidae]]. It is the second-most abundant and widespread storm petrel (after  [[Leach's storm petrel]]) and is the only bird in its family that is bluish-grey in colour.<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal|url=https://birdsna.org/Species-Account/bna/species/ftspet/introduction/|title=Fork-tailed Storm-Petrel - Introduction {{!}} Birds of North America Online|website=birdsna.org|doi=10.2173/bow.ftspet.01.1 |language=en|access-date=2018-09-19|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

The fork-tailed storm petrel is [[Pelagic zone|pelagic]], spending up to 8 months in the northern [[Pacific Ocean]]. They only return to land to breed, where they nest in a single [[Bird colony|colony]]. Their nests can be found in a rock crevice or small [[burrow]], where a single [[egg]] is laid. Their breeding range is along the coast of the northern Pacific Ocean, extending from northern [[California]] to northeast [[Palearctic|Asia]].<ref name=":03" />

They mainly feed on [[plankton]]ic [[crustacean]]s, small [[fish]], and [[squid]], but also consume [[offal]]. Similarly to other storm petrels, they forage by picking food off the surface of the water while in flight.<ref name=":03" />

== Taxonomy ==
The fork-tailed storm petrel was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1789 by the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] in his revised and expanded edition of [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the petrels in the [[genus]] ''[[Procellaria]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Procellaria furcata''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1789 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 2 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer | page=561 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2656054 }}</ref> Gmelin based his description on the "fork-tailed petrel" that had been described in 1785 in separate publications by the English ornithologist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] and the Welsh naturalist [[Thomas Pennant]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Latham | first=John | author-link=John Latham (ornithologist) | year=1785 | title=A General Synopsis of Birds | volume=3, Part 2 | publisher=Printed for Leigh and Sotheby | location=London | page=410, No. 16 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40079227 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last=Pennant | first=Thomas | author-link=Thomas Pennant | year=1785 | title=Arctic Zoology | volume=2 | publisher=Printed by Henry Hughs | location=London | page=535, No. 463 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32122321 }}</ref> It is now one of 18 species placed in the genus ''[[Hydrobates]]'' that was erected in 1822 by the German zoologist [[Friedrich Boie]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Boie  | first=Friedrich  | author-link=Friedrich Boie | year=1822 | title=Ueber Classification, insonderheit der europäischen Vogel | journal=Isis von Oken | volume=1822 | language=German | at=Cols 545–564 [562]| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27515512 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=August 2022 | title=Petrels, albatrosses | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/petrels/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 November 2022 }}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] '' hudro-'' meaning "water-" with ''batēs'' meaning "walker". The specific epithet ''furcatus'' is [[Latin]] meaning "forked".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n196/mode/1up 196], [https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n166/mode/1up 166]}}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognized:<ref name=ioc/>

* ''H. f. furcatus'' (Gmelin, JF, 1789) – northern population, breeds in [[Palearctic|northeast Asia]] to Alaska
* ''H. f. plumbeus'' ([[Titian Peale|Peale]], 1849)  – southern population, breeds in southeast Alaska south to northern California

The major differences between the two subspecies are slight size and [[plumage]] differences, where southern populations appear somewhat smaller and darker.<ref name=":1" /> Southern populations also begin and end their breeding season earlier than their northern counterparts.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Harris|first=W. Stanley|date=1974|title=Status, chronology and ecology of nesting storm petrels in northwestern California|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v076n03/p0249-p0261.pdf|journal=The Condor|volume=76|issue=3|pages=249–261|doi=10.2307/1366338|jstor=1366338}}</ref> However, no [[Molecular genetics|molecular genetic analysis]] has been conducted to discern the two subspecies.<ref name=":03" />

The species was formerly assigned to the genus ''[[Oceanodroma]]'' before that genus was synonymized with ''[[Northern storm petrel|Hydrobates]]''.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Taxonomic Updates – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/taxonomy/|access-date=2021-07-29|language=en-US}}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:Fork-tailed_Storm_Petrel,_St._Lazaria_Island,_Alaska.jpg|alt=|left|thumb|238x238px|The forked tail is more easily seen from above.]]
The fork-tailed storm petrel is small [[seabird]] that is 20 cm in length with a [[wingspan]] of 46 cm.<ref name=":03" /> Its foraging behaviour resembles other storm petrels, where it flies with short, stiff wingbeats close to the surface of the water.<ref name=":03" />

Despite its name, the forked tail in this bird is not always visible. The distinguishing feature of fork-tailed storm petrels is their overall bluish-gray [[plumage]]. They generally have a paler underside, which contrasts the darker colours under their wings. They also have a dark gray forehead, a black ear patch, and a small, black bill.<ref name=":03" /> Other storm petrels are significantly darker, such as the similar looking [[ashy storm petrel]].<ref name=":03" /> However, fork-tailed storm petrels from southern populations are somewhat darker than those in the north.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Grinnell|first1=J.|last2=Test|first2=Frederick H.|date=1939|title=Geographic Variation in the Fork-Tailed Petrel|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v041n04/p0170-p0172.pdf|journal=Condor|volume=41|issue=4|pages=170–172}}</ref>

Males and females are generally very similar in colour and size.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Boersma|first1=P. Dee|last2=Wheelwright|first2=Nathaniel T.|last3=Nerini|first3=Mary K.|last4=Wheelwright|first4=Eugenia Stevens|date=1980|title=The Breeding Biology of the Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrel (Oceanodroma furcata)|journal=The Auk|volume=97|issue=2|pages=268–282|jstor=4085701}}</ref> Juveniles also resemble adults with the exception of having a less noticeable notch in their tails.<ref name=":03" />

They are generally silent and only call when entering their [[Bird colony|breeding colony]] at night. Their most common call is a 3- to 5-note, raspy "ana-ana-ana", heard from both sexes. This is especially intense during [[Courtship display|courtship]], where their calls are accompanied by elaborate aerial displays.<ref name=":2" /> A higher-pitched, single note call is typically emitted by males, and it is used to locate females in noisy colonies and to maintain the pair bond.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Simons|first=Theodore R.|date=1981|title=Behavior and Attendance Patterns of the Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrel|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v098n01/p0145-p0158.pdf|journal=Auk|volume=98|issue=1|pages=145–158}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Fork_tail.jpg|thumb|The fork-tailed storm petrel frequents the open ocean and only returns to land to breed.]]
The range of the fork-tailed storm petrel is estimated to cover 22,400,000 km<sup>2</sup> with over 6,000,000 individuals occurring globally, making it the second-most widespread and abundant storm petrel.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/fork-tailed-storm-petrel-hydrobates-furcatus/details|title=Fork-tailed Storm-petrel (Hydrobates furcatus) - BirdLife species factsheet|website=datazone.birdlife.org|access-date=2018-09-20}}</ref>  During the [[breeding season]], the fork-tailed storm petrel form dense [[Bird colony|colonies]] on [[island]]s throughout northern [[California]], [[Oregon]], [[Washington (state)|Washington]], [[British Columbia]], [[Alaska]], and northeast [[Asia]] off the [[Kamchatka Peninsula]].<ref name=":03" /> Most of their population is concentrated in Alaska, notably in the [[Bering Sea]] near the [[Aleutian Islands]]. Their southernmost colony is on Little River Rock in [[Humboldt County, California|Humboldt County]], with a population of 200 individuals.<ref name=":3" /> It was reported in July 1939 that fork-tailed storm petrels were breeding on the Flannan Isles, Na h-Eileanan Flannach, which are about 35 km west of the northern tip of the Outer Hebrides, Scotland.<ref name=Flannan>The furthest Hebrides by E W Hodge p 187 in The Scottish Mountaineering Club Journal Vol 22 April 1940 No. 129.</ref>

Their nesting habitat varies, ranging from bare rock to forests. They typically build their nests under rock crevices or roots, or burrow into soft ground with low-growing vegetation.<ref name=":03" />

As they frequent the open ocean, little is known about fork-tailed storm petrels outside of the breeding season. They have been frequently sighted off the Californian coast, making them the most northerly distributed storm petrel during the winter.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Pelagic studies of seabirds in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean|last=Croissin|first=Richard S.|publisher=Smithsonian Institution Press|year=1974|editor-last=King|editor-first=Warren B.|location=Washington, D.C.|pages=154–205|chapter=The Storm Petrels (Hydrobatidae)}}</ref> Sparse sightings also indicate that they forage as far south as [[Hawaii]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gould|first=Patrick J.|date=1983|title=Seabirds between Alaska and Hawaii|journal=The Condor|volume=85|issue=3|pages=286–291|doi=10.2307/1367061|jstor=1367061}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==

=== Food and feeding===
Like other storm petrels, the fork-tailed storm petrel mainly feeds on [[crustacean]]s and [[fish]] near the surface of the ocean, including [[Amphipoda|amphipods]], [[Lanternfish|myctophids]], shallow-water fish (such as [[Hexagrammidae|greenling]] and [[sablefish]]), [[copepod]]s, [[Decapoda|decapods]], and [[squid]].<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Vermeer|first1=Kees|last2=Devito|first2=Kevin|last3=Rankin|first3=Leo|date=1988|title=Comparison of Nesting Biology of Fork-Tailed and Leach's Storm-Petrels|journal=Colonial Waterbirds|volume=11|issue=1|pages=46–57|doi=10.2307/1521169|jstor=1521169}}</ref> They are also extremely [[List of feeding behaviours|opportunistic]] and can be seen [[Scavenger|scavenging]] on [[Adipose tissue|fatty tissue]] of dead [[marine mammal]]s<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gill|first=Robert E.|date=1977|title=Unusual foraging by a fork-tailed storm-petrel|url=https://pubs.er.usgs.gov/publication/70185095|journal=The Auk|language=en|volume=94|issue=2|issn=1938-4254}}</ref> and also trailing behind fishing boats.<ref name=":03" />

Fork-tailed storm petrels have a well-developed [[olfactory system]] and heavily rely on [[Odor|odour]] to scout for food, so often they are the first birds to arrive at a pungent food source. When at the source, these birds seize their prey by fluttering across the water surface<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Verheyden|first1=Christophe|last2=Jouventin|first2=Pierre|date=1994|title=Olfactory Behavior of Foraging Procellariiforms|journal=The Auk|volume=111|issue=2|pages=285–291|doi=10.2307/4088593|jstor=4088593|doi-access=free}}</ref> and may occasionally dive to depths of 0.6 m.<ref name=":03" />

Like other Procellariiformes, the fork-tailed storm petrel produces [[stomach oil]] from its digested food and stores it in its [[proventriculus]], a section of the bird's [[Bird anatomy|digestive system]]. This oil permits these birds to go for a long time without food, but also allows them to transport nutrient-rich food back to their chicks from distant sources.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Roby|first1=Daniel D.|last2=Taylor|first2=Jan R. E.|last3=Place|first3=Allen R.|date=1997|title=Significance of Stomach Oil for Reproduction in Seabirds: An Interspecies Cross-Fostering Experiment|journal=The Auk|volume=114|issue=4|pages=725–736|doi=10.2307/4089292|jstor=4089292|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
[[File:The_aquatic_birds_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_(1906)_(14568897740).jpg|thumb|The fork-tailed storm petrel incubates a single egg in its burrow.]]
Like other species,  fork-tailed storm petrels spend most of their time out at sea and  only return to land to breed around late March to early April. To avoid [[predation]] and harassment by gulls, these birds only enter the colony at night and depart before sunrise.<ref name=":2" />

The fork-tailed storm petrel builds its nest in rock crevices or small [[burrow]]s on isolated [[island]]s.<ref name=":2" /> [[Courtship display|Courtship]] consists of vocalizations and aerial displays, often lasting for several weeks.<ref name=":4" /> Once established, pairs will remain together for the rest of the breeding season and if they are successful,  continually use the same nest year after year.<ref name=":2" /> Females will lay a single white egg directly on the floor of the burrow,<ref name="autogenerated1">":5">{{Cite journal|last1=DRUMMOND|last2=LEONARD|first2=B. A. &, M.L.|date=2009|title=Breeding biology of the Fork-tailed Storm-Petrel Oceanodroma furcata on Kasatochi Island, Aleutian Islands, Alaska|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/136280|journal=Marine Ornithology|volume=37|issue=3|pages=265–273}}</ref> and both parents will incubate it for around 50 days.<ref name=":2" /> Once the egg hatches, the adult  keeps the chick warm using its [[brood patch]] for the first five days.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Boersma|first=P. Dee|date=1986|title=Body Temperature, Torpor, and Growth in Chicks of Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrels (Oceanodroma furcata)|journal=Physiological Zoology|volume=59|issue=1|pages=10–19|jstor=30156084|doi=10.1086/physzool.59.1.30156084|s2cid=87048574}}</ref> Once able to [[Thermoregulation|thermoregulate]] for itself, the parents  then leave the chick, only returning to feed it one every one to four nights.<ref name=":4" /> After two months of slow growth, the petrel chick will finally [[fledge]] and leave the burrow.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=DRUMMOND|last2=LEONARD|first2=B. A. &, M.L.|date=2009|title=Breeding biology of the Fork-tailed Storm-Petrel Oceanodroma furcata on Kasatochi Island, Aleutian Islands, Alaska|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/136280|journal=Marine Ornithology|volume=37|issue=3|pages=265–273}}</ref>
[[File:Fork-tailed_storm_petrel_chick_(5699395374).jpg|thumb|A fork-tailed storm petrel chick]]
Living in areas with severe climatic conditions, fork-tailed storm petrels have many adaptations to ensure breeding success. Eggs can be left unattended  up to 7 days and still successfully hatch,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Boersma|first1=P. Dee|last2=Wheelwright|first2=Nathaniel T.|date=1979|title=Egg Neglect in the Procellariiformes: Reproductive Adaptations in the Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrel|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/102943|journal=Condor|volume=81|issue=2|pages=157–165|doi=10.2307/1367282|jstor=1367282|url-access=subscription}}</ref> whereas chick growth rates can be adjusted to being faster or slower depending on food supply, rather at a constant rate like many other birds.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Boersma|first1=P. Dee|last2=Parrish|first2=Julia K.|author-link2=Julia Parrish|date=1998|title=Flexible Growth Rates in Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrels: A Response to Environmental Variability|journal=The Auk|volume=115|issue=1|pages=67–75|doi=10.2307/4089112|jstor=4089112|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Interspecific interactions ===
The fork-tailed storm petrel is often the sole [[prey]] item for predators early in the breeding season because they return to these remote islands long before any other seabirds.<ref name=":03" /> These birds comprise a significant part of the diet of [[North American river otter|river otter]]s,<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=Quinlan|first=Susan E.|date=1983|title=Avian and River Otter Predation in a Storm-Petrel Colony|journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management|volume=47|issue=4|pages=1036–1043|doi=10.2307/3808162|jstor=3808162}}</ref> [[gull]]s,<ref name=":2" /> and [[Bird of prey|raptors]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=DeGange|first1=Anthony R.|last2=Nelson|first2=Jay W.|date=1982|title=Bald Eagle Predation on Nocturnal Seabirds|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|volume=53|issue=4|pages=407–409|jstor=4512766}}</ref> Further, introduced predators such as [[fox]]es, [[marten]]s, and [[raccoon]]s have a significant impact on breeding populations.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Drummond|first1=Brie A.|last2=Leonard|first2=Marty L.|date=2010|title=Reproductive Consequences of Nest Site Use in Fork-tailed Storm-Petrels in the Aleutian Islands, Alaska: Potential Lasting Effects of an Introduced Predator|journal=Avian Conservation and Ecology|language=en|volume=5|issue=2|doi=10.5751/ACE-00414-050204|issn=1712-6568|doi-access=free}}</ref> To defend themselves, fork-tailed storm petrels can eject their stomach oil at an incoming threat.<ref name=":03" />

Fork-tailed storm petrels generally coexist peacefully with other seabirds, where they can be seen sharing breeding habitat with [[tufted puffin]]s.<ref name=":2" /> In some cases, aggression can be observed, where they are known to steal food from [[Leach's storm petrel]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Morgan|first=K. H.|date=1994|title=Interspecific Kleptoparasitism by a Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrel (Oceanodroma furcata)|journal=Colonial Waterbirds|volume=17|issue=2|pages=187–188|doi=10.2307/1521299|jstor=1521299}}</ref>

== Human impacts and conservation ==
Being widespread and abundant, the fork-tailed storm petrel is not threatened with [[extinction]]. However, humans impact their life history in many ways.

As a species that feeds on surface material and follows ships, this storm petrel often ingests [[Oil spill|oil]] and [[plastic]] with their food items.<ref name=":03" /> Surprisingly, the fork-tailed storm petrel is relatively unaffected by the [[toxicity]] of oil, as their natural diet contains substances that are very chemically similar.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Boersma|first1=P. Dee|last2=Davies|first2=Emily M.|last3=Reid|first3=Walter V.|date=1988|title=Weathered crude oil effects on chicks of Fork-Tailed Storm-Petrels (Oceanodroma furcata)|journal=Archives of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology|language=en|volume=17|issue=4|pages=527–531|doi=10.1007/bf01055519|bibcode=1988ArECT..17..527B |s2cid=83734807|issn=0090-4341}}</ref> Plastics also do not severely affect the birds because they can be [[regurgitation (digestion)|regurgitated]] after [[ingestion]].<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://www.boem.gov/ESPIS/1/1399.pdf|title=Environmental Assessment of the Alaskan Continental Shelf|last=Boersma|first=Dee P.|publisher=U.S. Department of Commerce|year=1984|series=Final Reports of Principal Investigators|volume=22|pages=34–70|chapter=Storm-petrels as indicators of environmental conditions|access-date=2018-10-13|archive-date=2018-10-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181014010136/https://www.boem.gov/ESPIS/1/1399.pdf}}</ref>

In contrast, being a [[Apex predator|top marine predator]], fork-tailed storm petrels are susceptible to [[bioaccumulation]]. High [[lead]] concentrations have been found in the [[bone]]s of petrels,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Elliott|first1=John E.|last2=Scheuhammer|first2=Anton M.|date=1997|title=Heavy metal and metallothionein concentrations in seabirds from the Pacific coast of Canada|journal=Marine Pollution Bulletin|language=en|volume=34|issue=10|pages=794–801|doi=10.1016/S0025-326X(97)00034-9|bibcode=1997MarPB..34..794E |issn=0025-326X}}</ref> and [[DDT]] can cause [[eggshell]]s to become dangerously thin.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Henny|first1=C.J.|last2=Blus|first2=L.J.|last3=Prouty|first3=R.M.|date=1982|title=Organochlorine residues and shell thinning in Oregon seabird eggs|url=https://pubs.er.usgs.gov/publication/5221781|journal=Murrelet|language=en|volume=63|issue=1|pages=15–21|doi=10.2307/3535474|jstor=3535474|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

The greatest threats that face fork-tailed storm petrels today are [[global climate change]] and [[introduced species]]. An increased severity of storms makes  foraging more dangerous, decreasing the chances that an adult will return to the colony.<ref name=":6" /> The introduction of [[mammal]]s also adversely affect storm petrels, where [[raccoon]]s and [[North American river otter|river otter]]s prey upon nests,<ref name=":7" /> and [[rabbit]]s increase [[soil erosion]] and compromise the structural integrity of [[burrow]]s.<ref name=":03" />
